one_liner: A 26-year-old man presents with 5 days of hemoptysis, progressive shortness of breath, and dark 'cola-colored' urine.

stage2.hpi_extension: He started coughing up small amounts of bright red blood 5 days ago, followed by increasing dyspnea on exertion. This morning his urine was tea-colored. He has had fatigue and bilateral lower extremity swelling for a week. He denies cigarette use, but does work in an auto body shop with solvent exposure. No fevers, no rash, no sinus disease, no joint pain.
stage2.social_history: Auto body technician with solvent exposure. Former smoker, 5 pack-years, quit last month. No drugs.
stage2.physical_exam: General: young man in mild respiratory distress. HEENT: pale conjunctivae. CV: tachycardic, regular. Lungs: bilateral crackles with scattered rhonchi. Abdomen: soft, non-tender. Extremities: 1+ pretibial pitting edema. Skin: no rash, no purpura. Neuro: nonfocal.

lab_results.result: Anti-glomerular basement membrane (anti-GBM) antibodies: positive, high titer, directed against the alpha-3 chain of type IV collagen. ANCA (c-ANCA, p-ANCA): negative. ANA: negative. Complement C3/C4: normal.
lab_results.clinical_significance: Circulating anti-GBM antibodies to the alpha-3 chain of type IV collagen are diagnostic of anti-GBM disease / Goodpasture syndrome.

lab_results.result: Renal biopsy: light microscopy shows crescentic glomerulonephritis with cellular crescents in more than 60% of glomeruli. Immunofluorescence: smooth linear deposition of IgG along the glomerular basement membrane. Electron microscopy: no electron-dense immune complex deposits.
lab_results.clinical_significance: Linear IgG staining along the GBM with crescentic glomerulonephritis is the pathognomonic histologic finding of anti-GBM disease.

lab_results.result: 3+ blood, 2+ protein. Microscopy: dysmorphic RBCs and red blood cell casts. Creatinine 3.6 (baseline unknown, presumed normal).
lab_results.clinical_significance: RBC casts with dysmorphic red cells define a glomerulonephritic (nephritic) picture, and the rapid rise in creatinine indicates rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis (RPGN).

stage5.final_clue: He is started on plasmapheresis, high-dose corticosteroids, and cyclophosphamide; his pulmonary hemorrhage resolves over days and his creatinine slowly improves over weeks.

debrief.classic_presentation: Anti-GBM disease (Goodpasture syndrome when both lung and kidney are involved) classically presents in young men in their 20s-30s with the combination of pulmonary hemorrhage (hemoptysis, dyspnea, bilateral alveolar infiltrates) and rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis (hematuria, RBC casts, rising creatinine). Cigarette smoking and hydrocarbon exposure are recognized triggers that unmask the pulmonary component by exposing alveolar basement membrane antigens.
debrief.pathophysiology: Anti-GBM disease is caused by autoantibodies directed against the alpha-3 chain of type IV collagen (the Goodpasture antigen), which is found in the basement membranes of glomeruli and pulmonary alveoli. Antibody binding activates complement and recruits neutrophils, producing necrotizing, crescentic glomerulonephritis and alveolar hemorrhage. Immunofluorescence shows a characteristic smooth, linear pattern of IgG along the glomerular basement membrane, distinguishing it from immune-complex (granular) and pauci-immune (ANCA-associated) RPGN.
debrief.treatment_overview: Treatment must be initiated urgently to prevent irreversible kidney failure. First-line therapy is plasmapheresis to remove circulating antibodies combined with corticosteroids and cyclophosphamide to suppress new antibody formation. Supportive care includes treatment of pulmonary hemorrhage and avoidance of further smoking and solvent exposure. Prognosis depends heavily on the creatinine at presentation; patients who are already dialysis-dependent rarely recover renal function.
high_yield_pearls: Linear IgG staining along the GBM on immunofluorescence is pathognomonic for anti-GBM disease.
Target antigen is the alpha-3 chain of type IV collagen in the GBM and alveolar basement membrane.
Classic presentation: young man with hemoptysis plus RPGN — Goodpasture syndrome.
Three patterns of crescentic RPGN: linear IgG (anti-GBM), granular (immune complex, e.g., lupus), pauci-immune (ANCA+).
Treatment is plasmapheresis plus corticosteroids plus cyclophosphamide; smoking worsens pulmonary involvement.
sources: First Aid for the USMLE Step 1 (2017) — Renal chapter, 'Nephritic syndrome — Goodpasture syndrome (anti-GBM disease)' section.
